content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 10,
1993
Mr. Kirk Pessner
, Merksamer,
Parrinello, Mueller
& Naylor
591 Redwood Highway,
#4000
Mill Valley,
CA
94941
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—93—288
Dear Mr. Pessner:
You have requested confirmation of advice provided to you
concerning the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act’) •1/
Because you have not identified the person on
whose behalf you have requested this advice,
we provide the
following informal response to your question.
2
’
Your letter of July 21,
1993, accurately summarizes the
advice provided to you.
In order for a law firm to qualify as a
lobbying firm, the firm must receive or become entitled to receive
$5,000 or more during a calendar quarter from a single client and
a partner or employee of the firm must engage in direct
communication with a public official for the purpose of
influencing legislative or administrative action on behalf of the
client.
(Sections 82038.5 and 18238.5(a).)
Therefore,
in your
example, if during a calendar quarter a law firm receives $3,000
for lobbying activities from one client and $3,000 from another
client for such activities, the law firm will not qualify as a
lobbying firm as it did not receive $5,000 from a single client.
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c)-(3) .)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807
•
(916) 322-5660


1—93—288
2
However,
once a law firm qualifies as a lobbying firm,
any
person who makes a payment, regardless of the amount, to the firm
for lobbying services is a lobbyist employer and becomes subject
to the registration and disclosure requirements under the Act.
(Section 82039.5, Regulation 18614.,
and Pessner Advice Letter,
No.
1—87—174.)
Sinc,rely,
U
Lynda Cassady
Assistant Division
‘ief
Technical Assistance Division

sections.question_synthetic: When does a law firm qualify as a lobbying firm under the Political Reform Act, and what threshold amount must be received from a single client for such qualification?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A law firm qualifies as a lobbying firm only when it receives or becomes entitled to receive $5,000 or more during a calendar quarter from a single client AND a partner or employee engages in direct communication with a public official to influence legislative or administrative action. Once qualified, any person paying the firm for lobbying services, regardless of amount, becomes a lobbyist employer subject to registration and disclosure requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is informal advice response; no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections with headers present; legal question and conclusion extracted from body text. Footnotes indicate this is informal assistance without immunity protections of formal written advice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: When does a law firm qualify as a lobbying firm under the Political Reform Act, and what threshold amount must be received from a single client for such qualification?

CONCLUSION: A law firm qualifies as a lobbying firm only when it receives or becomes entitled to receive $5,000 or more during a calendar quarter from a single client AND a partner or employee engages in direct communication with a public official to influence legislative or administrative action. Once qualified, any person paying the firm for lobbying services, regardless of amount, becomes a lobbyist employer subject to registration and disclosure requirements.

In order for a law firm to qualify as a lobbying firm, the firm must receive or become entitled to receive $5,000 or more during a calendar quarter from a single client and a partner or employee of the firm must engage in direct communication with a public official for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action on behalf of the client. (Sections 82038.5 and 18238.5(a).) Therefore, in your example, if during a calendar quarter a law firm receives $3,000 for lobbying activities from one client and $3,000 from another client for such activities, the law firm will not qualify as a lobbying firm as it did not receive $5,000 from a single client. Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. (Section 82039.5, Regulation 18614., and Pessner Advice Letter, No. 1—87—174.) Sinc,rely, U Lynda Cassady Assistant Division ‘ief Technical Assistance Division
embedding.summary: FPPC informal advice letter confirming guidance on lobbying firm qualification thresholds under the Political Reform Act, specifically addressing the $5,000 single-client quarterly requirement and subsequent registration obligations for lobbyist employers.
